title: Dog Cherry Eye: Red Lump in Eye Corner Treatment & Triage
meta_desc: Find out what a cherry red lump in your dog's eye means. Learn about Cherry Eye symptoms, urgency levels, and why a photo is vital for vet triage.
content_html:
What is the Red Lump in My Dog's Eye?

A cherry-red lump appearing in the inner corner of your dog's eye is most likely a prolapsed gland of the third eyelid, commonly referred to as 'Cherry Eye.' Dogs have a third eyelid that houses a significant tear-producing gland. When the connective tissue holding this gland in place weakens or fails, the gland pops out, appearing as a fleshy, red, bulbous mass.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for this condition is Medium. While Cherry Eye is generally not a life-threatening emergency, it is not a condition that will resolve on its own. The exposed gland can become dry, irritated, and prone to secondary infections. If left untreated, it can lead to long-term issues like 'dry eye' (keratoconjunctivitis sicca). You should contact your veterinarian to schedule an exam within 24 to 48 hours.

Why Taking a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a clear, high-resolution photo of the eye can be an invaluable tool for your veterinary team. A photo allows the vet to see the exact state of the gland, check for signs of discharge or corneal cloudiness, and monitor if the size of the lump changes over time. This visual data helps the clinic determine how quickly your pet needs to be seen and helps them prepare the appropriate diagnostic tools before you even arrive.

faqs.question: Can I treat Cherry Eye at home with eye drops?
faqs.answer: While some drops may reduce inflammation, they will not fix the underlying structural issue. Professional veterinary treatment, often surgery, is usually required to reposition the gland.

faqs.question: Is Cherry Eye painful for my dog?
faqs.answer: Initially, it may not be painful, but it can be uncomfortable. If the dog rubs at the eye, they can cause painful corneal ulcers or infections.

faqs.question: Which dog breeds are most prone to this?
faqs.answer: Brachycephalic (flat-faced) breeds like Bulldogs, Pugs, and Frenchies, as well as Beagles and Cocker Spaniels, are genetically more susceptible to this condition.
